name: Cognitive Psychology
description: An in-depth exploration of mental processes including attention, perception, memory, language, problem solving, and decision making. Students examine classic experiments and contemporary research to understand how the mind organizes, stores, and retrieves information.

modules.title: History of Cognitive Psychology
modules.objectives: Trace the cognitive revolution from behaviorism to information-processing models; identify key figures including Neisser, Miller, and Broadbent.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 7.1: What is Cognition
modules.videoNote: CrashCourse Psychology #2 — Psychological Research & the Cognitive Revolution
modules.video2Note: The History of Cognitive Psychology — Overview Lecture
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — History of Psychology Practice
modules.assignment: Write a timeline essay tracing the shift from behaviorism to cognitive psychology. Include at least four key figures and their contributions. (350 words)

modules.title: Perception & Attention
modules.objectives: Explain selective, divided, and sustained attention; describe bottleneck theories; apply findings from the cocktail party effect and change blindness.
modules.videoNote: Daniel Simons — The Invisible Gorilla (Inattentional Blindness Demo)
modules.assignment: Conduct the invisible gorilla attention experiment informally with 5 participants. Report your findings and explain using attentional theories. (300 words)

modules.title: Memory Systems
modules.objectives: Distinguish sensory, short-term, and long-term memory; compare the Atkinson-Shiffrin model with Baddeley's working memory model.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 8.1: How Memory Functions
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Memory Encoding & Storage
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Memory Encoding & Storage Practice
modules.assignment: Create a diagram comparing the Atkinson-Shiffrin model with Baddeley's working memory model. Annotate each component's function and capacity. (with 200-word explanation)

modules.title: Working Memory
modules.objectives: Describe Baddeley's components of working memory (phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, episodic buffer, central executive); explain its role in reasoning and comprehension.
modules.video2Note: Working Memory and Learning — Educational Overview
modules.assignment: Test your own working memory capacity using a digit-span task. Compare your results to published norms and explain implications for learning. (300 words)

modules.title: Long-term Memory
modules.objectives: Differentiate explicit (declarative: episodic and semantic) from implicit (procedural, priming) memory; explain consolidation and the role of the hippocampus.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 8.2: Brain & Long-term Memory
modules.videoNote: Types of Long-term Memory — Explicit vs. Implicit
modules.assignment: Give three examples each of episodic, semantic, and procedural memories from your own life. Analyze how each was encoded and what retrieval cues help access them. (350 words)

modules.title: Forgetting
modules.objectives: Explain Ebbinghaus's forgetting curve; distinguish decay theory from interference theory; describe retrieval failure and motivated forgetting.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 8.3–8.4: Memory Storage & Forgetting
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Forgetting & Amnesia
modules.assignment: Apply spaced repetition principles to a list of 20 vocabulary words. Track your retention over 3 days. Analyze how your forgetting curve compares to Ebbinghaus's. (300 words)

modules.title: Mental Imagery
modules.objectives: Describe mental rotation and image scanning research; evaluate whether mental images are pictorial or propositional; apply imagery to memory enhancement.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 7.1: Concepts & Mental Imagery
modules.videoNote: Mental Rotation and Spatial Reasoning — Cognitive Psychology
modules.assignment: Take a mental rotation test online and reflect on your performance. What does spatial ability research tell us about individual differences in mental imagery? (300 words)

modules.title: Language & Thought
modules.objectives: Explain the structure of language (phonemes, morphemes, syntax, semantics); evaluate the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis; describe Chomsky's universal grammar.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Language & Thought
modules.assignment: Research one study on linguistic relativity (e.g., color perception across languages). Summarize the study and discuss whether language shapes thought or vice versa. (350 words)

modules.title: Problem Solving
modules.objectives: Describe algorithmic vs. heuristic problem-solving strategies; identify obstacles including mental set and functional fixedness; explain insight and incubation.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Problem Solving & Creativity
modules.assignment: Solve the Duncker candle problem and explain your thought process. Analyze which obstacles (mental set, functional fixedness) you encountered and how you overcame them. (300 words)

modules.title: Decision Making
modules.objectives: Apply Kahneman's System 1 vs. System 2 thinking; identify major cognitive biases including anchoring, representativeness, and availability; explain prospect theory.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 7.3–7.4: Judgment & Decision Making
modules.videoNote: Daniel Kahneman — Thinking, Fast and Slow Overview
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Judgment & Decision Making Practice
modules.assignment: Identify and analyze three real-world examples of cognitive biases from news headlines. Explain how each bias could lead to poor decision-making. (350 words)

modules.title: Expertise & Skill Acquisition
modules.objectives: Describe the stages of skill learning (novice to expert); explain deliberate practice and chunking; compare expert vs. novice problem-solving approaches.
modules.videoNote: Anders Ericsson — The Science of Expertise & Deliberate Practice
modules.video2Note: CrashCourse Study Hall — Expertise and Learning
modules.assignment: Interview someone who is an expert in a skill. Analyze how they developed expertise using concepts of deliberate practice, chunking, and the 10,000-hour rule. (350 words)

modules.title: Cognitive Development
modules.objectives: Apply Piaget's four stages to cognitive development; evaluate Vygotsky's sociocultural theory; describe information-processing changes across the lifespan.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 9.2: Cognitive Development (Piaget & Vygotsky)
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy AP Psychology — Piaget's Stages
modules.assignment: Design and describe a conservation task to test a 4-year-old and a 7-year-old. Predict results based on Piaget's stages and explain the theoretical implications. (350 words)

modules.title: Computational Models of Cognition
modules.objectives: Compare connectionist/parallel distributed processing models with symbolic AI models; evaluate the computer metaphor for the human mind.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 7.1: Cognition & Information Processing
modules.videoNote: Connectionism and Neural Networks in Cognitive Science
modules.assignment: Compare the human brain to a computer. Identify at least three ways they are similar and three ways they fundamentally differ. What are the limitations of the computer metaphor? (350 words)

modules.title: Applied Cognition
modules.objectives: Apply cognitive principles to real-world settings including education, human-computer interaction, eyewitness testimony, and clinical interventions.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e — Ch 8.4: Enhancing Memory & Applied Cognition
modules.videoNote: Elizabeth Loftus — Eyewitness Memory & the Justice System
modules.assignment: Apply three evidence-based cognitive strategies (e.g., spaced repetition, retrieval practice, interleaving) to improve your studying for one week. Reflect on effectiveness. (400 words)

quizQuestions.question: The cognitive revolution emerged primarily as a reaction against:
quizQuestions.answer: Behaviorism
quizQuestions.explanation: The cognitive revolution in the 1950s-60s was a rejection of behaviorism's exclusive focus on observable behavior.

quizQuestions.question: George Miller's famous 1956 paper identified short-term memory capacity as approximately:
quizQuestions.answer: 7 items (plus or minus 2)
quizQuestions.explanation: Miller's 'Magic Number Seven, Plus or Minus Two' described the limited capacity of short-term memory.

quizQuestions.question: Ulric Neisser is significant in cognitive psychology for:
options: Writing the first cognitive psychology textbook
quizQuestions.answer: Writing the first cognitive psychology textbook
quizQuestions.explanation: Neisser's 1967 book 'Cognitive Psychology' helped legitimize and define the field.

quizQuestions.question: The information-processing model of the mind compares cognition to:
quizQuestions.answer: A computer
quizQuestions.explanation: The information-processing metaphor compares the mind to a computer that inputs, processes, stores, and outputs information.

quizQuestions.question: Donald Broadbent's filter model proposed that:
options: All sensory information is processed in parallel
A bottleneck filters information before conscious processing
Memory and attention are the same process
quizQuestions.answer: A bottleneck filters information before conscious processing
quizQuestions.explanation: Broadbent's filter model proposed that a selective filter screens sensory information by physical characteristics before higher processing.

quizQuestions.question: The cocktail party effect demonstrates:
options: Selective attention to relevant stimuli in a noisy environment
The inability to process two conversations simultaneously
quizQuestions.answer: Selective attention to relevant stimuli in a noisy environment
quizQuestions.explanation: The cocktail party effect shows we can selectively attend to our name even in a crowded, noisy setting.

quizQuestions.question: Change blindness refers to:
options: The inability to perceive color changes
Failure to notice large changes in a scene when attention is elsewhere
A medical condition affecting peripheral vision
quizQuestions.answer: Failure to notice large changes in a scene when attention is elsewhere
quizQuestions.explanation: Change blindness demonstrates that we do not automatically encode all aspects of a visual scene.

quizQuestions.question: The inattentional blindness experiment by Simons and Chabris showed:
options: Observers miss unexpected objects when focused on a primary task
Vision is processed entirely in parallel
quizQuestions.answer: Observers miss unexpected objects when focused on a primary task
quizQuestions.explanation: When focused on counting basketball passes, many participants failed to notice a person in a gorilla suit walking through.

quizQuestions.question: Treisman's attenuation model differs from Broadbent's filter model in that:
options: Unattended information is weakened rather than completely blocked
It applies only to visual attention
quizQuestions.answer: Unattended information is weakened rather than completely blocked
quizQuestions.explanation: Treisman proposed that the filter attenuates (weakens) rather than eliminates unattended messages, allowing important information through.

quizQuestions.question: Divided attention refers to:
options: The ability to attend to multiple tasks simultaneously
quizQuestions.answer: The ability to attend to multiple tasks simultaneously
quizQuestions.explanation: Divided attention involves processing multiple sources of information at the same time, with performance costs.

quizQuestions.question: Sensory memory holds information for approximately:
options: Less than a second to a few seconds
quizQuestions.answer: Less than a second to a few seconds
quizQuestions.explanation: Sensory memory (iconic and echoic) holds exact copies of sensory input for very brief durations before it decays.

quizQuestions.question: The Atkinson-Shiffrin model is also known as the:
quizQuestions.answer: Multi-store model
quizQuestions.explanation: Atkinson and Shiffrin's 1968 model proposed three distinct memory stores: sensory, short-term, and long-term.

quizQuestions.question: Rehearsal in the Atkinson-Shiffrin model serves to:
options: Transfer information from STM to LTM
quizQuestions.answer: Transfer information from STM to LTM
quizQuestions.explanation: Maintenance rehearsal recirculates information in STM; elaborative rehearsal encodes it into LTM.

quizQuestions.question: George Sperling's partial report procedure demonstrated:
options: That iconic memory holds more information than previously thought
quizQuestions.answer: That iconic memory holds more information than previously thought
quizQuestions.explanation: Sperling showed that the iconic memory store holds a large amount of information, but it decays rapidly.

quizQuestions.question: A criticism of the Atkinson-Shiffrin model is that:
options: It treats STM as a unitary passive store rather than an active system
It focuses only on verbal memory
quizQuestions.answer: It treats STM as a unitary passive store rather than an active system
quizQuestions.explanation: Baddeley and Hitch argued that STM is better conceptualized as an active working memory system with multiple components.

quizQuestions.question: The phonological loop in Baddeley's working memory model handles:
quizQuestions.answer: Verbal and auditory information
quizQuestions.explanation: The phonological loop temporarily stores and rehearses verbal/auditory information (like repeating a phone number).

quizQuestions.question: The central executive in Baddeley's model functions as:
options: The sole source of memory capacity
quizQuestions.answer: An attentional controller coordinating subsystems
quizQuestions.explanation: The central executive directs attention and controls the slave systems (phonological loop and visuospatial sketchpad).

quizQuestions.question: The word-length effect demonstrates that:
options: Working memory capacity is affected by how long words take to say
Short words are harder to chunk
Rehearsal does not help working memory
quizQuestions.answer: Working memory capacity is affected by how long words take to say
quizQuestions.explanation: Longer words take more time to rehearse, reducing the number that can be held in the phonological loop.

quizQuestions.question: The episodic buffer in Baddeley's model was added to account for:
options: The integration of information across modalities into coherent episodes
quizQuestions.answer: The integration of information across modalities into coherent episodes
quizQuestions.explanation: Added in 2000, the episodic buffer integrates information from the phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, and LTM into unified episodes.

quizQuestions.question: Working memory capacity is strongly correlated with:
quizQuestions.answer: Reading comprehension and reasoning ability
quizQuestions.explanation: Higher working memory capacity predicts better performance on complex cognitive tasks including reading and reasoning.

quizQuestions.question: Episodic memory contains memories of:
quizQuestions.answer: Personal autobiographical events
quizQuestions.explanation: Episodic memory stores personally experienced events with their temporal and spatial context.

quizQuestions.question: Priming is best classified as:
quizQuestions.answer: Implicit memory
quizQuestions.explanation: Priming — a prior exposure facilitating later processing — is an implicit, unconscious form of memory.

quizQuestions.question: The case of patient H.M. demonstrated that the hippocampus is essential for:
quizQuestions.answer: Forming new declarative memories
quizQuestions.explanation: After hippocampal removal, H.M. could not form new explicit (declarative) memories but retained procedural learning.

quizQuestions.question: Memory consolidation refers to:
options: The stabilization of a memory trace over time
quizQuestions.answer: The stabilization of a memory trace over time
quizQuestions.explanation: Consolidation is the process by which newly encoded memories are stabilized, often occurring during sleep.

quizQuestions.question: Semantic memory stores:
options: General knowledge and facts about the world
quizQuestions.answer: General knowledge and facts about the world
quizQuestions.explanation: Semantic memory contains conceptual knowledge about the world independent of personal experience.

quizQuestions.question: Ebbinghaus's forgetting curve shows that forgetting is:
options: Rapid initially then slowing over time
quizQuestions.answer: Rapid initially then slowing over time
quizQuestions.explanation: Ebbinghaus's forgetting curve shows steep initial forgetting that decelerates as time passes.

quizQuestions.question: Proactive interference occurs when:
options: New memories interfere with old ones
Old memories interfere with new learning
quizQuestions.answer: Old memories interfere with new learning
quizQuestions.explanation: Proactive interference: old information makes it harder to learn or remember new information.

quizQuestions.question: Retrograde amnesia results in:
options: Loss of memories prior to brain injury
quizQuestions.answer: Loss of memories prior to brain injury
quizQuestions.explanation: Retrograde amnesia causes loss of memories formed before the injury, while anterograde amnesia affects new memory formation.

quizQuestions.question: The encoding specificity principle states that recall is best when:
quizQuestions.answer: Retrieval context matches encoding context
quizQuestions.explanation: Tulving's encoding specificity principle: memory is aided when the context at retrieval matches the context at encoding.

quizQuestions.question: Motivated forgetting (Freudian repression) refers to:
options: Unconsciously pushing threatening memories out of awareness
quizQuestions.answer: Unconsciously pushing threatening memories out of awareness
quizQuestions.explanation: Freud proposed repression as a defense mechanism that keeps threatening or painful memories unconscious.

quizQuestions.question: Shepard and Metzler's mental rotation experiments showed that:
options: Images are stored in verbal codes
Rotation time increases linearly with the angle of rotation
Visual imagery uses different brain areas than perception
quizQuestions.answer: Rotation time increases linearly with the angle of rotation
quizQuestions.explanation: Response time increased proportionally with the degree of rotation, suggesting people mentally rotate images in a continuous, analog way.

quizQuestions.question: The pictorial view of mental imagery (Kosslyn) proposes that:
options: Images are stored as abstract propositions
Mental images are depictive, spatial representations
Images are reconstructed from semantic memory
quizQuestions.answer: Mental images are depictive, spatial representations
quizQuestions.explanation: Kosslyn argued mental images are quasi-pictorial, activating visual cortex areas similar to actual perception.

quizQuestions.question: The method of loci technique works by:
options: Linking information to mental images placed in familiar locations
quizQuestions.answer: Linking information to mental images placed in familiar locations
quizQuestions.explanation: The method of loci encodes information by imagining it placed at specific locations along a familiar mental route.

quizQuestions.question: Mental scanning experiments by Kosslyn demonstrated that:
options: It takes longer to scan greater distances in a mental image
Visual imagery does not activate the visual cortex
quizQuestions.answer: It takes longer to scan greater distances in a mental image
quizQuestions.explanation: Kosslyn found participants took longer to scan larger distances in imagined maps, suggesting images have spatial properties.

quizQuestions.question: Paivio's dual-coding theory states that memory is improved when information is:
options: Encoded in both verbal and visual codes
quizQuestions.answer: Encoded in both verbal and visual codes
quizQuestions.explanation: Paivio's dual-coding theory: information encoded both verbally and visually is better remembered than verbally only.

quizQuestions.question: A morpheme is best defined as:
options: The smallest unit of sound in a language
The smallest unit of meaning in a language
The meaning of a word in context
quizQuestions.answer: The smallest unit of meaning in a language
quizQuestions.explanation: Morphemes are the smallest linguistic units that carry meaning (e.g., 'un-', 'happy', '-ness').

quizQuestions.question: Chomsky's universal grammar hypothesis states that:
options: All languages share the same vocabulary roots
Humans have an innate capacity for language structure
Only spoken language follows grammar rules
quizQuestions.answer: Humans have an innate capacity for language structure
quizQuestions.explanation: Chomsky proposed that all humans are biologically equipped with a universal grammar framework for language acquisition.

quizQuestions.question: The strong Sapir-Whorf hypothesis would mean that:
options: Language has no effect on thought
Speakers of different languages perceive reality differently due to their language
Thought develops before language in children
quizQuestions.answer: Speakers of different languages perceive reality differently due to their language
quizQuestions.explanation: The strong Sapir-Whorf hypothesis posits that the language we speak determines how we think and perceive the world.

quizQuestions.question: Syntax refers to:
quizQuestions.answer: Rules governing sentence structure
quizQuestions.explanation: Syntax describes the grammatical rules that specify how words can be combined to form sentences.

quizQuestions.question: Research on color naming across languages suggests:
options: Language has no effect on color perception
Linguistic categories can influence color discrimination speed
quizQuestions.answer: Linguistic categories can influence color discrimination speed
quizQuestions.explanation: Research shows that having specific color terms in a language can speed up discrimination of those colors.

quizQuestions.question: An algorithm in problem solving is:
options: A step-by-step procedure guaranteed to reach a solution
quizQuestions.answer: A step-by-step procedure guaranteed to reach a solution
quizQuestions.explanation: Algorithms are systematic, exhaustive procedures that guarantee a correct solution if followed correctly.

quizQuestions.question: Functional fixedness refers to:
options: Being unable to see new solutions due to rigid thinking about objects' uses
Failing to remember relevant past solutions
The tendency to confirm initial hypotheses
quizQuestions.answer: Being unable to see new solutions due to rigid thinking about objects' uses
quizQuestions.explanation: Functional fixedness limits problem solving by preventing people from seeing an object's potential uses beyond its typical function.

quizQuestions.question: Insight is best characterized by:
options: A sudden restructuring of the problem leading to a solution
quizQuestions.answer: A sudden restructuring of the problem leading to a solution
quizQuestions.explanation: Insight involves a sudden shift in how a problem is represented, leading to immediate recognition of the solution.

quizQuestions.question: Means-ends analysis involves:
options: Working to reduce the gap between current state and goal state
quizQuestions.answer: Working to reduce the gap between current state and goal state
quizQuestions.explanation: Means-ends analysis is a heuristic that identifies the difference between current and goal states and takes actions to reduce it.

quizQuestions.question: The incubation effect in problem solving refers to:
options: Solving problems faster under time pressure
Improvement after taking a break from the problem
Using analogical reasoning from another domain
quizQuestions.answer: Improvement after taking a break from the problem
quizQuestions.explanation: Incubation allows unconscious processing to continue; people often return to problems with fresh insight after a break.

quizQuestions.question: Kahneman's System 1 thinking is characterized as:
quizQuestions.answer: Fast, automatic, and intuitive
quizQuestions.explanation: System 1 operates automatically and quickly with little effort, often relying on heuristics and associations.

quizQuestions.question: The anchoring bias occurs when:
options: People ignore all information except the first
An initial piece of information disproportionately influences subsequent judgments
People choose options with higher perceived value
quizQuestions.answer: An initial piece of information disproportionately influences subsequent judgments
quizQuestions.explanation: Anchoring: people rely too heavily on the first number or piece of information encountered when making decisions.

quizQuestions.question: Prospect theory demonstrates that people are:
options: More motivated to avoid losses than to achieve equivalent gains
Always risk-seeking when facing potential losses
Unaffected by how choices are framed
quizQuestions.answer: More motivated to avoid losses than to achieve equivalent gains
quizQuestions.explanation: Prospect theory shows that the pain of losing something is typically twice as powerful as the pleasure of gaining the same thing.

quizQuestions.question: The representativeness heuristic leads people to:
options: Judge based on how easily examples come to mind
Judge probability based on how similar something is to a prototype
Rely on the first information received
quizQuestions.answer: Judge probability based on how similar something is to a prototype
quizQuestions.explanation: The representativeness heuristic judges probability by how well something matches a mental prototype, often ignoring base rates.

quizQuestions.question: Overconfidence bias refers to:
options: Having excessive confidence in the accuracy of one's judgments
quizQuestions.answer: Having excessive confidence in the accuracy of one's judgments
quizQuestions.explanation: Overconfidence bias leads people to be more certain of their knowledge and predictions than their actual accuracy warrants.

quizQuestions.question: Chunking in skill acquisition refers to:
options: Grouping individual elements into meaningful units processed as a single item
quizQuestions.answer: Grouping individual elements into meaningful units processed as a single item
quizQuestions.explanation: Experts chunk information — combining individual elements into larger, meaningful units — increasing effective working memory capacity.

quizQuestions.question: Deliberate practice is distinguished from regular practice by:
options: Focusing on challenging specific weaknesses with immediate feedback
Being enjoyable and motivating by design
quizQuestions.answer: Focusing on challenging specific weaknesses with immediate feedback
quizQuestions.explanation: Deliberate practice requires working just beyond one's current ability level with focused attention and corrective feedback.

quizQuestions.question: Expert-novice differences in problem solving show that experts:
options: Represent problems at a deeper principle-based level
quizQuestions.answer: Represent problems at a deeper principle-based level
quizQuestions.explanation: Experts categorize problems by underlying principles while novices focus on surface features, allowing experts to choose better strategies.

quizQuestions.question: Automaticity in skill learning means:
options: Tasks can be performed with minimal conscious effort after extensive practice
Skills learned automatically cannot be verbalized
quizQuestions.answer: Tasks can be performed with minimal conscious effort after extensive practice
quizQuestions.explanation: Automaticity develops through extensive practice, freeing up working memory resources for higher-level processing.

quizQuestions.question: The 10,000-hour rule suggests that:
options: Anyone can master any skill in exactly 10,000 hours
Extensive deliberate practice is needed for world-class expertise
Natural talent determines expertise regardless of practice
quizQuestions.answer: Extensive deliberate practice is needed for world-class expertise
quizQuestions.explanation: Ericsson's research found top performers in many domains practice for approximately 10,000 hours of deliberate practice.

quizQuestions.question: Piaget's formal operational stage (approximately age 12+) is characterized by:
options: Symbolic thinking but inability to conserve
quizQuestions.answer: Abstract, hypothetical, and logical reasoning
quizQuestions.explanation: In the formal operational stage, adolescents can think abstractly, reason hypothetically, and systematically test solutions.

quizQuestions.question: Vygotsky's concept of scaffolding refers to:
options: Temporary support provided by a more knowledgeable person to help a learner succeed
quizQuestions.answer: Temporary support provided by a more knowledgeable person to help a learner succeed
quizQuestions.explanation: Scaffolding provides just enough help to allow learners to perform tasks within their ZPD, which is gradually withdrawn as competence grows.

quizQuestions.question: Egocentrism in Piaget's preoperational stage means children:
options: Cannot understand that others have different perspectives
Focus only on one dimension of problems
quizQuestions.answer: Cannot understand that others have different perspectives
quizQuestions.explanation: Preoperational egocentrism means children assume others see, hear, and feel things the same way they do.

quizQuestions.question: A schema in Piaget's theory is:
options: A memory of a specific event
A mental framework for understanding the world
quizQuestions.answer: A mental framework for understanding the world
quizQuestions.explanation: Schemas are organized mental structures or frameworks that help us interpret and interact with the world.

quizQuestions.question: Information-processing theories view cognitive development as:
options: Gradual increases in processing speed, capacity, and strategy use
quizQuestions.answer: Gradual increases in processing speed, capacity, and strategy use
quizQuestions.explanation: Information-processing approaches view development as continuous improvements in memory capacity, processing speed, and cognitive strategies.

quizQuestions.question: Connectionist (PDP) models of cognition propose that knowledge is:
options: Distributed across networks of interconnected units
Identical to how digital computers store data
quizQuestions.answer: Distributed across networks of interconnected units
quizQuestions.explanation: Connectionist models represent knowledge as patterns of activation across many interconnected processing units simultaneously.

quizQuestions.question: The Turing Test tests whether:
options: A computer can process information faster than humans
A machine's responses are indistinguishable from a human's in conversation
quizQuestions.answer: A machine's responses are indistinguishable from a human's in conversation
quizQuestions.explanation: The Turing Test assesses artificial intelligence by seeing whether a computer can converse in ways indistinguishable from a human.

quizQuestions.question: A strength of computational models of cognition is:
options: They allow precise, testable predictions about cognitive processes
They require no assumptions about brain mechanisms
They capture all of human consciousness
quizQuestions.answer: They allow precise, testable predictions about cognitive processes
quizQuestions.explanation: Computational models generate specific, falsifiable predictions and can simulate cognitive processes to test theoretical claims.

quizQuestions.question: A key limitation of the computer metaphor for the mind is that:
options: The brain is massively parallel and embodied unlike standard serial computers
quizQuestions.answer: The brain is massively parallel and embodied unlike standard serial computers
quizQuestions.explanation: The human brain operates through massively parallel distributed processes and is deeply connected to the body, unlike traditional computers.

quizQuestions.question: The frame problem in artificial intelligence refers to:
options: Difficulty processing visual frames in video
The challenge of representing what does NOT change in a situation
Computational limits of working memory models
quizQuestions.answer: The challenge of representing what does NOT change in a situation
quizQuestions.explanation: The frame problem involves determining which aspects of a situation remain unchanged when an action occurs — difficult for AI systems.

quizQuestions.question: Retrieval practice (testing effect) benefits learning because:
options: Actively retrieving information strengthens memory traces more than re-reading
It increases working memory capacity permanently
quizQuestions.answer: Actively retrieving information strengthens memory traces more than re-reading
quizQuestions.explanation: The testing effect shows that practicing retrieval (e.g., self-quizzing) produces stronger long-term retention than passive review.

quizQuestions.question: Elizabeth Loftus's eyewitness research showed that:
options: Leading questions can create false memories in eyewitnesses
Emotional stress always enhances memory accuracy
Eyewitnesses recall events like video recordings
quizQuestions.answer: Leading questions can create false memories in eyewitnesses
quizQuestions.explanation: Loftus demonstrated that post-event misleading information can be incorporated into eyewitness memory.

quizQuestions.question: Spaced (distributed) practice is superior to massed practice because:
options: Spacing allows forgetting and retrieval strengthening long-term retention
It is easier to maintain motivation
quizQuestions.answer: Spacing allows forgetting and retrieval strengthening long-term retention
quizQuestions.explanation: Spacing practice over time forces retrieval from longer-term stores, consolidating memories more effectively than cramming.

quizQuestions.question: Human-factors psychology applies cognitive research to:
options: Designing systems and tools that match human cognitive capabilities
quizQuestions.answer: Designing systems and tools that match human cognitive capabilities
quizQuestions.explanation: Human factors (ergonomics) applies cognitive psychology to design interfaces, controls, and systems that reduce error and cognitive load.

quizQuestions.question: Interleaving (mixing practice of different topics) improves:
options: Long-term retention and transfer to new problems
quizQuestions.answer: Long-term retention and transfer to new problems
quizQuestions.explanation: Interleaved practice requires discrimination between problem types, which strengthens long-term learning and transfer, though it feels harder.

questions.question: The cognitive revolution was primarily a reaction against which school of psychology?
questions.answer: Behaviorism
questions.explanation: The cognitive revolution in the 1950s-60s rejected behaviorism's exclusive focus on observable behavior, reintroducing mental processes as scientific subjects.

questions.question: George Miller's 'magic number' for short-term memory capacity is approximately:
questions.answer: 7 items (plus or minus 2)
questions.explanation: Miller found that short-term memory holds approximately 7 (plus or minus 2) chunks of information.

questions.question: The cocktail party effect demonstrates:
options: Inability to attend to any background noise
Selective attention to one's name in a noisy environment
Parallel processing of all auditory stimuli
The limit of working memory capacity
questions.answer: Selective attention to one's name in a noisy environment
questions.explanation: The cocktail party effect shows we selectively filter relevant stimuli (like our name) even while attending to other things.

questions.question: The central executive in Baddeley's working memory model serves as:
options: An attentional controller coordinating the subsystems
questions.answer: An attentional controller coordinating the subsystems
questions.explanation: The central executive directs attention and supervises the phonological loop and visuospatial sketchpad.

questions.question: Sperling's partial report procedure showed that iconic memory:
options: Holds more information than can be fully recalled
questions.answer: Holds more information than can be fully recalled
questions.explanation: By cuing participants to report specific rows after stimulus offset, Sperling showed iconic memory holds more than participants can fully report.

questions.question: Encoding specificity states that recall is best when:
questions.answer: Retrieval context matches encoding context
questions.explanation: Tulving's encoding specificity: memory is strongest when the conditions at retrieval match those present during encoding.

questions.question: Shepard and Metzler's mental rotation studies suggested that mental images are:
options: Not processed in the visual system
questions.answer: Analog and spatially represented
questions.explanation: Rotation time increased linearly with angle of rotation, suggesting mental images behave like physical objects being rotated.

questions.question: Functional fixedness is an obstacle to problem solving because it:
options: Prevents seeing novel uses for familiar objects
Causes people to forget relevant information
questions.answer: Prevents seeing novel uses for familiar objects
questions.explanation: Functional fixedness restricts thinking about objects to their conventional uses, blocking creative problem solutions.

questions.question: Chomsky's universal grammar proposes that:
options: All languages share identical grammar rules
Children have an innate predisposition to acquire language structure
questions.answer: Children have an innate predisposition to acquire language structure
questions.explanation: Chomsky proposed a biologically innate language acquisition device that provides children with a universal grammar framework.

questions.question: The case of patient H.M. demonstrated that the hippocampus is critical for:
questions.answer: Forming new explicit memories
questions.explanation: After bilateral hippocampal removal, H.M. could not form new declarative (explicit) memories.

questions.question: Ebbinghaus's forgetting curve shows that forgetting is:
options: Most rapid shortly after learning then slowing
Faster for emotionally neutral material only
questions.answer: Most rapid shortly after learning then slowing
questions.explanation: Ebbinghaus showed that the most forgetting occurs shortly after learning, with the rate slowing as time progresses.

questions.question: Kahneman's System 1 thinking is best described as:
questions.answer: Fast, automatic, and intuitive
questions.explanation: System 1 operates automatically with little conscious effort, using intuition and heuristics for fast judgments.

questions.question: Deliberate practice differs from ordinary practice in that it:
options: Focuses on stretching abilities beyond current limits with specific feedback
questions.answer: Focuses on stretching abilities beyond current limits with specific feedback
questions.explanation: Deliberate practice targets specific weaknesses at the edge of one's competence with immediate corrective feedback.

questions.question: Piaget's formal operational stage is distinguished by:
options: Ability to think abstractly and hypothetically
questions.answer: Ability to think abstractly and hypothetically
questions.explanation: During formal operations (age 12+), adolescents can reason about hypothetical scenarios, abstract concepts, and systematic logic.

questions.question: A connectionist (PDP) model represents knowledge as:
options: Activation patterns distributed across interconnected units
questions.answer: Activation patterns distributed across interconnected units
questions.explanation: Connectionist models represent knowledge as distributed patterns of activation across networks of simple processing units.

questions.question: The testing effect demonstrates that:
options: Re-reading is the most effective study strategy
Retrieval practice produces stronger long-term retention than passive review
Tests cause anxiety that impairs consolidation
Spaced practice only works for declarative memory
questions.answer: Retrieval practice produces stronger long-term retention than passive review
questions.explanation: Actively retrieving information during study (testing effect) strengthens memory traces more effectively than passive re-reading.

questions.question: Loftus's misinformation effect research showed that:
options: Memory is immune to post-event suggestion
Leading questions can alter eyewitness memories
Emotional events are always remembered accurately
Repressed memories can be reliably recovered
questions.answer: Leading questions can alter eyewitness memories
questions.explanation: Loftus demonstrated that misleading post-event information can become incorporated into eyewitness memory.

questions.question: Spaced practice is superior to massed practice because:
options: It forces repeated retrieval over time strengthening long-term retention
questions.answer: It forces repeated retrieval over time strengthening long-term retention
questions.explanation: Spaced practice distributes learning across time, requiring retrieval from progressively longer intervals, which consolidates memory.

questions.question: The representativeness heuristic often leads to neglect of:
questions.answer: Base rate information (statistical probability)
questions.explanation: People using representativeness ignore statistical base rates when judging probability based on how well something matches a prototype.

questions.question: Priming is classified as implicit memory because:
options: It influences behavior without conscious awareness
It is stored in the hippocampus
It can only be tested verbally
questions.answer: It influences behavior without conscious awareness
questions.explanation: Priming facilitates processing of related information without the person being aware that the prior exposure is influencing them.

questions.question: Vygotsky's scaffolding concept implies that effective instruction should:
options: Always let students discover solutions independently
Provide permanent support for all learning tasks
Target tasks just above the learner's current independent level
questions.answer: Target tasks just above the learner's current independent level
questions.explanation: Scaffolding within the ZPD provides appropriate support for tasks the learner cannot yet do independently, gradually withdrawing help as competence grows.

questions.question: The word-length effect in working memory shows that:
options: Longer words are easier to remember
Fewer long words can be held in the phonological loop than short words
Word length affects only semantic memory
Working memory capacity is unlimited for familiar words
questions.answer: Fewer long words can be held in the phonological loop than short words
questions.explanation: Long words take longer to subvocally rehearse, reducing the number that can be maintained in the phonological loop.

questions.question: Interleaving different types of problems compared to blocked practice results in:
options: Better immediate performance but similar long-term retention
Worse immediate performance but better long-term retention and transfer
No difference in either immediate or long-term performance
Better performance only for simple skills
questions.answer: Worse immediate performance but better long-term retention and transfer
questions.explanation: Interleaving feels harder and produces lower immediate performance, but forces retrieval and discrimination between strategies, improving long-term learning.

questions.question: Retrograde amnesia involves:
options: Inability to form new long-term memories
Loss of memories formed before a brain injury
questions.answer: Loss of memories formed before a brain injury
questions.explanation: Retrograde amnesia affects memories that existed prior to the brain injury, while anterograde amnesia affects new memory formation.

questions.question: The method of loci memory technique leverages:
options: Spatial mental imagery linked to a familiar route
questions.answer: Spatial mental imagery linked to a familiar route
questions.explanation: The method of loci encodes information by placing mental images along a well-known spatial route, exploiting spatial memory strengths.

questions.question: Prospect theory demonstrates that people are:
options: More motivated to avoid losses than to achieve equivalent gains
Always risk-seeking when facing potential losses
Unaffected by how choices are framed
questions.answer: More motivated to avoid losses than to achieve equivalent gains
questions.explanation: Kahneman and Tversky's prospect theory shows that losses psychologically loom about twice as large as equivalent gains.

questions.question: Expert problem solvers differ from novices primarily in that they:
options: Categorize problems by deep structural principles
Rely more on algorithms than heuristics
questions.answer: Categorize problems by deep structural principles
questions.explanation: Experts represent problems at a deeper, principle-based level while novices focus on surface features, enabling better strategy selection.

questions.question: Broadbent's filter model of attention proposes that:
options: All sensory information is fully processed before filtering
A selective filter blocks most information based on physical characteristics early in processing
Unattended stimuli are weakened but not completely blocked
questions.answer: A selective filter blocks most information based on physical characteristics early in processing
questions.explanation: Broadbent's early selection model proposes that a bottleneck filter selects information based on physical properties before semantic processing.

questions.question: The dual-coding theory (Paivio) suggests that memory is improved when information is:
options: Encoded in both verbal and visual/imagistic codes
questions.answer: Encoded in both verbal and visual/imagistic codes
questions.explanation: Paivio's dual-coding theory states that information encoded both verbally and visually creates richer, more retrievable memory traces.

questions.question: The incubation effect in problem solving is best explained by:
options: Unconscious processing and forgetting of misleading mental sets
Simple physical recovery from mental fatigue
questions.answer: Unconscious processing and forgetting of misleading mental sets
questions.explanation: Incubation likely involves unconscious processing and forgetting of misleading mental sets, both contributing to eventual insight.

questions.question: A key criticism of the Turing Test as a measure of machine intelligence is:
options: It is too difficult for any machine to pass
Verbal behavior that mimics intelligence does not necessarily involve understanding
It requires machines to have emotional experience
It can only test mathematical ability
questions.answer: Verbal behavior that mimics intelligence does not necessarily involve understanding
questions.explanation: Searle's Chinese Room argument shows that passing the Turing Test does not prove genuine understanding or consciousness.

questions.question: Human factors (ergonomics) psychology primarily applies cognitive research to:
options: Designing systems that fit human cognitive abilities and limitations
questions.answer: Designing systems that fit human cognitive abilities and limitations
questions.explanation: Human factors psychology uses cognitive research to design safer, more efficient tools, interfaces, and work environments.

questions.question: Proactive interference in memory occurs when:
options: New information interferes with old memories
Old information interferes with learning new information
Retrieval cues are absent at test
questions.answer: Old information interferes with learning new information
questions.explanation: Proactive interference: previously learned information makes it harder to learn or remember subsequently learned material.

questions.question: Sapir-Whorf (linguistic relativity) research on color perception suggests:
options: Having more color terms in a language speeds discrimination between those colors
questions.answer: Having more color terms in a language speeds discrimination between those colors
questions.explanation: Research shows that fine-grained linguistic color categories can facilitate faster perceptual discrimination between colors in those categories.

questions.question: The episodic buffer in Baddeley's working memory model was added to explain:
options: Visual manipulation of objects in mental space
Integration of information from multiple sources into coherent episodes
The capacity limits of sensory memory
questions.answer: Integration of information from multiple sources into coherent episodes
questions.explanation: The episodic buffer integrates information from the phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, and long-term memory into unified, multi-modal episodes.
